topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice sat on the kitchen counter, crisp white paper mocking the warm, stale air of my apartment. I traced the bold letters with a fingertip, the edges feeling brittle under my touch. Every time I looked at the campus map tacked above the fridge, a familiar ache tightened in my chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made my breath catch. I walked over to the window, staring out at the familiar quad, but everything looked strangely muted, washed out by a sudden, unwelcome grayness. My hands began to tremble slightly, and I had to grip the edge of the counter to keep them steady. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet room. "This isn't happening."

[story 2]

It was the silence that was the worst. The usual low hum of activity—the distant laughter, the hurried footsteps across the lawn—had evaporated, leaving behind a vacuum that pulled at my attention. I sat on the worn leather of my desk chair, staring blankly at my laptop screen, though the words swam before my eyes. A slow, hot prickle started at the base of my neck, spreading outward. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but my thumb hovered over the call button, paralyzed by a strange inability to move. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the feeling down, but a tightness clenched around my jaw, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the final paragraph, the bureaucratic jargon seeming to mock the reality of the situation. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy surrender to the weight of the news. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my living room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at strands as if to physically pull the feeling out of my system. My voice, when I finally spoke, was surprisingly flat, devoid of its usual energy. "So, that's it," I murmured to the empty room, the words tasting like ash. I sank onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands, the fabric of the cushion rough against my skin as I just held still.
